elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we develop and implement an efficient asymptotic-preserving (AP) scheme to solve the gas mixture of Boltzmann equations, under the so-called ``relaxation time scale" relevant to the epochal relaxation phenomenon.
elements.sent-2411-002: The disparity in molecular masses, ranging across several orders of magnitude, leads to significant challenges in both the evaluation of collision operators and designing of efficient numerical schemes in order to capture the multi-scale nature of the dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-003: A direct implementation by using the spectral method faces prohibitive computational costs as the mass ratio decreases due to the need to resolve vastly different thermal velocities.. Different from [I.
elements.sent-2411-006: Sci., 17 (2019), pp. 1257–1289], we propose an alternative approach by conducting asymptotic expansions for the collision operators, which can significantly reduce the computational complexity and works well for uniformly small $\varepsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-007: By incorporating the separation of three time scales in the model's relaxation process [P.. Degond and B.
elements.sent-2411-009: Sci., 6 (1996), pp. 405–436], we design an AP scheme that is able to capture the epochal relaxation phenomenon of disparage mass mixtures while maintaining the computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-010: Numerical experiments will demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed scheme in handling large mass ratios of heavy and light species, in addition to validating the AP properties.
elements.sent-2411-011: The numerical computation of gas mixtures with disparate masses is very challenging, with important applications in plasma physics and aerospace engineering.
elements.sent-2411-012: In practice, mass ratios of light and heavy molecules usually span several orders of magnitude, from $10^2$ to $10^5$, as seen in spacecraft re-entry plasma environments \cite{Brun2012 , ITER fusion reactor dust-plasma interactions \cite{Takase , and evaporation/condensation processes \cite{Takata-Golse .}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: In kinetic theory, the Boltzmann equation models the evolution of rarefied gases, with the multi-species Boltzmann equation describing gas mixtures.
elements.sent-2411-014: Major challenges for solving the Boltzmann equation include the non-local collision operator of an integral type and treatment of the multiple scales.
elements.sent-2411-015: Over the decades, deterministic methods, being free of statistical noise, are particularly advantageous for solving the Boltzmann equation, especially in near-continuum flows \cite{Dimarco-Pareschi .}
elements.sent-2411-016: In particular, the Fourier spectral method has been popularly used since the pioneer work \cite{Pareschi-Perthame, Pareschi-Russo , with fast spectral method developed afterwards, see for examples \cite{Mouhot-Pareschi, Filbet-Mouhot-Pareschi, Gamba-Haack, Gamba-Haack-Hauck-Hu .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: For gas mixtures, recent studies have achieved notable progress \cite{Jaiswal-Alexeenko-Hu, WuLei .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Compared to single-species or standard multi-species cases, gas mixtures with disparate masses pose substantial computational challenges in handling collision operators.
elements.sent-2411-019: A key characteristic of these mixtures is the large disparity in species’ thermal velocities: the computational domain must accommodate the fastest species, while grid spacing must resolve the slowest.
elements.sent-2411-020: As a result, direct implementations of spectral methods would have a computational complexity that scales with the mass ratio \cite{Jaiswal-Alexeenko-Hu, WuLei .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Various studies address these challenges through adaptive meshing in velocity space \cite{Taitano-Chacon-Simakov and asymptotic models tailored to large mass ratios \cite{Charles-Desvillettes, Taitano-Chacon-Simakov .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Besides the computational challenges for the nonlinear collision operators, the dynamics of the gas mixture leads to a more complex physics.
elements.sent-2411-023: We distinguish the ``heavy'' species (denoted by `H') and the ``light'' species (denoted by `L').
elements.sent-2411-024: Define the square-rooted mass ratio between these two species: $\displaystyle\varepsilon = \sqrt{m^L/m^H}$, and the disparate mass regime refers to the case when $\varepsilon \ll 1$.
elements.sent-2411-025: Back in the 1960s, the epochal relaxation phenomenon was first pointed out by Grad \cite{Grad , who noticed that the relaxation rate of the light species is faster than that of the heavy species.}
elements.sent-2411-026: There has been several work along this line, see \cite{Braginskii,Spitzer-Harm,Goldman-Sirovich,Chmieleski-Ferziger,Johnson,Petit-Darrozes,Degond-Lucquin-Desreux .}
elements.sent-2411-027: In particular, Degond and Lucquin-Desreux \cite{Degond-Lucquin-Desreux, Degond-Lucquin-Desreux-2, Degond_bookchapter characterized the relaxation process in binary gas mixtures through a separation of three time-scales and derived their corresponding asymptotic limits.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Under the longest time scale, both particle distributions reach thermal equilibrium, with their temperatures evolving toward each other which is governed by a relaxation equation.
elements.sent-2411-029: This is the asymptotic regime we are particularly interested in this work.
elements.sent-2411-030: With the distinctive stiffness in our model \eqref{rescaled-Boltz under the above-mentioned longest time scale, designing an efficient asymptotic-preserving (AP) scheme becomes a challenging task, especially in the disparate mass case when $\varepsilon \ll 1$.}
elements.sent-2411-031: The goal of Asymptotic-Preserving (AP) schemes \cite{Jin-AP is to avoid resolving the small scaling parameter numerically while preserving the macroscopic behavior of the model at the discretized level.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Several AP schemes have been designed for the Boltzmann mixture model, for examples see \cite{Jin-Shi,Jin-Li .}
elements.sent-2411-033: However, none of those work considered the particular time scalings for the disparate mass models as we focus on in this work.
elements.sent-2411-034: We mention that in \cite{Gamba-Jin-Liu , the authors studied the same model and employed the ansatzes $f^{L,H = f_0^{L,H + \varepsilon f_1^{L,H}$ to design a system for four variables $f_0^{L,H}$, $f_1^{L,H}$; they only gave a theoretical proof that the numerical method was AP but fail to carry out implementations, given their scheme was overly complicated and seemed computationally infeasible in practice.}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Novelty and contributions: Compared to this earlier work \cite{Gamba-Jin-Liu , we propose a significantly simpler and novel numerical approach.}
elements.sent-2411-036: To the best of our knowledge, this current work is the {\it first to provide an efficient, computationally cheap AP scheme that can accurately capture the ``epochal relaxation" phenomenon for the Boltzmann mixture model with disparate mass, yet without suffering the computational cost even if $\varepsilon \ll 1$.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Numerical results have demonstrated the feasibility and effectiveness of our new method.
elements.sent-2411-038: Besides, another main novelty of our work lies in the efficient computation of inter-particle collision operators.
elements.sent-2411-039: In \cite{Degond-Lucquin-Desreux , the authors derived the scaling of equations as a function of the mass ratio of the particle, expanded the collision operators in powers of the mass ratio and showed the dynamics of the mixtures at various time scales.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Inspired by but different from that theoretical work, we mainly contribute in the following aspects: (i) Our expansion is different from \cite{Degond-Lucquin-Desreux since we incorporate a lower-order collision operator term ($Q_2$) to account for the slowest time scale.}

elements.sent-2411-042: This approach significantly reduces the computational costs compared to straightforward spectral type methods, achieving a {\it uniform efficiency even when the mass ratio is extremely small.}
elements.sent-2411-043: The rest of the paper is organized as the following: Section \ref{sec:model introduces the gas mixture of Boltzmann equation under different time scales and reviews its key properties in the disparate mass regime.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Section \ref{sec:AE carries out asymptotic approximations for the inter-particle collision operators, introduces our carefully designed asymptotic-expansion method and compares the computational efficiency with the standard spectral approach.}
elements.sent-2411-045: In Section \ref{sec:AP , we present the AP time discretization and its theoretical analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-046: In Section \ref{sec:numerics , we show the robustness and efficiency of our proposed AP scheme in the numerical examples, comparing with the brutal-force spectral method and the time evolution of macroscopic quantities solved by the relaxation model when $\varepsilon$ is small.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Lastly, we conclude the paper in Section \ref{sec:conclusion .}

elements.sent-2411-230: In this study, we developed asymptotic-preserving schemes for the Boltzmann mixture model with disparate mass.
elements.sent-2411-231: Our approach circumvents the prohibitive computational costs of traditional spectral methods by conducting asymptotic expansions in order to approximate the collision operators, especially when the mass disparity is huge.
elements.sent-2411-232: We consider the space-homogeneous problem and consider the longest time scale among the three time scales.
elements.sent-2411-233: Our AP scheme can accurately capture the epochal relaxation phenomenon, without resolving the small scaling parameter characterized by the square-root of mass ratio.

elements.sent-2411-236: In this section, we provide a brief overview of the spectral method for evaluating the scaled operators $Q^{LH _\varepsilon$ and $Q^{HL _\varepsilon$.}}
elements.sent-2411-237: This approach is an adaptation of the fast spectral method developed by \cite{Jaiswal-Alexeenko-Hu (hereafter referred to as the Jaiswal-Alexeenko-Hu method), with necessary modifications to account for the effects of scaling.}
elements.sent-2411-238: To address the scaling issue, our approach is to first reverse the scaling of the solutions, allowing the use of the Jaiswal-Alexeenko-Hu method to evaluate the collision operators.
elements.sent-2411-239: Once the collision operators have been computed, we then reapply the scaling to the resulting solutions.
elements.sent-2411-240: We first perform a set of variable changes \begin{equation}\label{equation:change_variables} \tilde{v}^{H} = \varepsilon v^{H}, \quad f^{H}(v^{H}) = \varepsilon^{d_{v}} \tilde{f}^{H}(\tilde{v}^{H}), \quad Q^{HL}_{\varepsilon}(f^{H}, f^{L})(v^{H}) = \varepsilon^{d_{v}} \tilde{Q}^{HL}_{\varepsilon}(\tilde{v}^{H}),\end{equation} to get \begin{equation}\label{equation:QLH_unscaled} \begin{aligned} Q^{LH}_{\varepsilon}(v^{L}) = \sqrt{1 + \varepsilon^2} \int_{\mathbb{R}^{d_{v}} \times \mathbb{S}^{d_{v}-1}} B^{LH}\left(\tfrac{|g^{LH}|}{\sqrt{1 + \varepsilon^2}}, \sigma \cdot \hat{g}^{LH}\right) \times \left[ f^{L}(v^{\prime L}) \tilde{f}^{H}(\tilde{v}^{\prime H}) - f^{L}(v^{L}) \tilde{f}^{H}(\tilde{v}^{H}) \right] \mathrm{d}\sigma \mathrm{d}g^{LH}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} with collision rules \begin{equation}\left\{ \begin{aligned} v^{\prime L} = v^{L} - \frac{1}{1 + \varepsilon^2} g^{LH} + \frac{1}{1 + \varepsilon^2} |g^{LH}| \sigma, \tilde{v}^{\prime H} = v^{L} - \frac{1}{1 + \varepsilon^2} g^{LH} - \frac{\varepsilon^2}{1 + \varepsilon^2} |g^{LH}| \sigma, \end{aligned} \right.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-271: In this paper, we develop and implement an efficient asymptotic-preserving (AP) scheme to solve the gas mixture of Boltzmann equations under the disparate mass scaling relevant to the so-called ``epochal relaxation'' phenomenon.
elements.sent-2411-272: The disparity in molecular masses, ranging across several orders of magnitude, leads to significant challenges in both the evaluation of collision operators and the designing of time-stepping schemes to capture the multi-scale nature of the dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-273: A direct implementation of the spectral method faces prohibitive computational costs as the mass ratio increases due to the need to resolve vastly different thermal velocities.. Unlike [I.
elements.sent-2411-274: Sci., 17 (2019), pp. 1257-1289], we propose an alternative approach based on proper truncation of asymptotic expansions of the collision operators, which significantly reduces the computational complexity and works well for small $\varepsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-275: Sci., 6 (1996), pp. 405-436], we design an AP scheme that captures the specific dynamics of the disparate mass model while maintaining computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-276: Numerical experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed scheme in handling large mass ratios of heavy and light species, as well as capturing the epochal relaxation phenomenon.
elements.sent-2411-277: The numerical computation of gas mixtures with disparate masses is a challenging task, with important applications in plasma physics and aerospace engineering.
elements.sent-2411-278: In kinetic theory, the Boltzmann equation models the evolution of rarefied gases and their mixtures.
elements.sent-2411-279: Over the decades, deterministic methods, being free of statistical noise, have proven particularly advantageous for solving the Boltzmann equation, especially in near-continuum flows \cite{Dimarco-Pareschi .}
elements.sent-2411-280: In particular, the Fourier spectral method has been popularly used since the pioneering work \cite{Pareschi-Perthame, Pareschi-Russo , with fast spectral methods developed afterwards, see, for example, \cite{Mouhot-Pareschi, Filbet-Mouhot-Pareschi, Gamba-Haack, Gamba-Haack-Hauck-Hu .}}
elements.sent-2411-281: Compared to single-species or standard multi-species cases, gas mixtures with disparate masses bring substantial computational challenges in the handling of collision operators.
elements.sent-2411-282: A key characteristic of these mixtures is the large disparity in thermal velocities of different species: the grid spacing must resolve both the fastest and the slowest species.
elements.sent-2411-283: As a result, direct implementations of Fourier spectral methods would have a computational complexity that scales with the mass ratio \cite{Jaiswal-Alexeenko-Hu, WuLei .}
elements.sent-2411-284: Back in the 1960s, early efforts were made to derive the hydrodynamic models for plasmas and binary gas mixtures, the epochal relaxation phenomenon in disparate mass mixtures was first pointed out by Grad \cite{Grad , who remarked that the relaxation rate of the light species is faster than that of the heavy species by a factor depending on the square-root of the mass ratio $\varepsilon = \sqrt{m^L/m^H}$ where $L$ and $H$ denote the light and heavy species, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-285: There have been several analysis works along this line, see \cite{Braginskii,Spitzer-Harm,Goldman-Sirovich,Chmieleski-Ferziger,Johnson,Petit-Darrozes,Degond-Lucquin-Desreux .}
elements.sent-2411-286: In particular, Degond and Lucquin-Desreux \cite{Degond_bookchapter, Degond-Lucquin-Desreux, Degond-Lucquin-Desreux-2 characterized the epochal relaxation process by identifying three distinct time scales described by $\tau = 1$, $\tau = \varepsilon$ and $\tau = \varepsilon^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-287: In contrast to normal mixture models, the disparate mass mixture exhibits scale separation at both kinetic and macroscopic levels, which brings significant computational challenges besides calculation for collision operators.
elements.sent-2411-288: Specifically, at the kinetic level, the distribution functions of different species Maxwellize on distinct time scales; however at the macroscopic level, the velocities and temperatures of different species relax on separate time scales.
elements.sent-2411-289: To capture the epochal relaxation phenomenon in numerical simulation, an efficient scheme not only needs to handle the stiffness brought by the small parameters $\varepsilon$ and $\tau$, but also allow efficient transition between kinetic and macroscopic regimes.
elements.sent-2411-290: To this end, we mention Asymptotic-Preserving (AP) schemes~\cite{Jin-AP , whose goal is to avoid resolving the small scaling parameter numerically while preserving the asymptotic limits from the kinetic to the macroscopic models in the discrete setting.}
elements.sent-2411-291: Such schemes allow automatic switches from a kinetic solver to the macroscopic solver.
elements.sent-2411-292: However, none of those work considered the epochal relaxation dynamics of disparate mass models.
elements.sent-2411-293: The main focus of this work is to design an AP scheme for the Boltzmann mixture model with disparate masses that can capture the epochal relaxation phenomenon.
elements.sent-2411-294: To deal with the velocity disparity in the computation of inter-particle collision operators, we propose a novel approach that is based on proper truncation on the asymptotic expansion given in \cite{Degond_bookchapter,Degond-Lucquin-Desreux .}
elements.sent-2411-295: This reduces the computation to the evaluation of several velocity space integrals and derivatives.
elements.sent-2411-296: Similar approach has been used in~\cite{Taitano-Chacon-Simakov .}
elements.sent-2411-297: To deal with the time stiffness, the BGK-penalty idea~\cite{Filbet-Jin is employed.}
elements.sent-2411-298: We mention that in \cite{Gamba-Jin-Liu , the authors studied the same model and employed the ansatze $f^{L,H = f_0^{L,H + \varepsilon f_1^{L,H}$ to formulate a system of equations for the variables $f_0^{L,H}$ and $f_1^{L,H}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-299: They provided a theoretical AP proof of their method but lacked a practical implementation.
elements.sent-2411-300: Plus, their scheme still relies on the Fourier spectral method for the evaluation of inter-particle collision operators, which is computationally infeasible in practice.
elements.sent-2411-301: To the best of our knowledge, this is the {\it first work in developing an efficient asymptotic-preserving scheme that can capture the so-called ``epochal relaxation" phenomenon for the Boltzmann mixture model with disparate mass, without suffering from the time stiffness when the square-root of the mass ratio $\varepsilon \ll 1$.}
elements.sent-2411-302: In particular, we first derive the truncated model based on the asymptotic expansion in \cite{Degond_bookchapter,Degond-Lucquin-Desreux and adopt the BGK-penalization technique by \cite{Filbet-Jin , which requires updating the macroscopic quantities in the moment equations.}}
elements.sent-2411-303: Then, we carefully design an implicit scheme yet can be implemented explicitly for the moment equations, while ensuring stability and being able to capture the epochal relaxation phenomenon.
elements.sent-2411-304: Lastly, based on a careful analysis, we show the consistency of moments update and the asymptotic-preserving property of our kinetic scheme.
elements.sent-2411-305: Another major novelty and contribution of our work is developing a {\it uniformly efficient numerical approach to evaluate the inter-particle collision operators when the mass ratio is small.}
elements.sent-2411-306: Inspired by the expansion in \cite{Degond-Lucquin-Desreux but from a numerical perspective, we design an effective way to compute the ``truncated" inter-particle collision operators and solve the mixture model.}
elements.sent-2411-307: Specifically, we invent a dual-grid procedure to calculate the angular integrals for the asymptotic light-heavy collision operators, which involves a careful polar-grid design and interpolation between the polar and Cartesian grids.
elements.sent-2411-308: To this end, we provide an alternative and faster method compared to the existing expensive Fourier spectral scheme for disparate mass mixtures (see Remark 3 in~\cite{Jaiswal-Alexeenko-Hu ).}
elements.sent-2411-309: The rest of the paper is organized as follows: In Section \ref{sec:model , we introduce the Boltzmann gas mixture with disparate mass scaling and list some key properties.}
elements.sent-2411-310: Section \ref{sec:AE describes the asymptotic expansion method for evaluating inter-particle collision operators, the polar grid design and angular integration, in addition to the applicability of our AE method in small $\varepsilon$ regime.}
elements.sent-2411-311: In Section \ref{sec:AP , we present the time discretization, show the consistency of moments update and most importantly, the AP property of our numerical scheme.}
elements.sent-2411-312: In Section \ref{sec:numerics , we demonstrate the robustness and efficiency of the proposed scheme by several numerical experiments, which focus on the effectiveness of the asymptotic expansion and how the epochal relaxation phenomenon is captured.}
elements.sent-2411-313: The conclusion is summarized in Section \ref{sec:conclusion .}

elements.sent-2411-333: Specifically, there is a three-time scale separation in the disparate mass regime.
elements.sent-2411-334: The fastest time scale ($\tau = 1$): collision time of the light species.
elements.sent-2411-335: The heavy particle distribution does not evolve in time and the light particle one is described by a kinetic equation with two collision terms corresponding to self collisions and an elastic scattering of the light particles against the heavy ones as if the latter were steady.
elements.sent-2411-336: Also, the relaxation of velocities occurs at this time scale.
elements.sent-2411-337: The intermediate time scale ($\tau = \varepsilon$): collision time of the heavy species.
elements.sent-2411-338: The light particles are at thermodynamic equilibrium with a centered Maxwellian distribution, while the heavy ones are subject to collisions with particles of the same species.
elements.sent-2411-339: The slowest time scale ($\tau = \varepsilon^2$): relaxation time scale.
elements.sent-2411-340: Both distributions are thermalized and the temperatures evolve one to each other via a relaxation equation.

elements.subsection-2411-019: Approximation of the inter-particle collision operators
elements.sent-2411-441: In this test, we assess the AE method and the SP method as candidates for approximating inter-particle collision operators in our problem.

elements.sent-2411-443: Convergence study of the AE and SP methods.
elements.sent-2411-444: We first study the convergence in the velocity variable of the numerical solutions computed by the AE and SP methods.
elements.sent-2411-445: In view of the discussion in Section~\ref{subsec:AE_applicability , the two methods operate differently in $\varepsilon$ regimes: the AE method is expected to be accurate only for small $\varepsilon$, while the SP method requires an increasingly larger $N_v$ as $\varepsilon$ decreases.}
elements.sent-2411-446: Therefore, for the AE method, we study four cases of gas mixtures characterized by $\varepsilon = 0.2$, $\varepsilon = 0.1$, $\varepsilon = 0.03$, and $\varepsilon = 0.01$ with velocity discretization by $N_v=30$, $60$, $120$, $240$, and $480$ for all $\varepsilon$ values.
elements.sent-2411-447: For the SP method, we study four cases of gas mixtures characterized by $\varepsilon = 0.1$, $\varepsilon = 0.2$, $\varepsilon = 0.5$, and $\varepsilon = 1$.
elements.sent-2411-448: According to Proposition \ref{Prop:SP , for each $\varepsilon$, we need to use velocity discretizations proportional to $1/\varepsilon$.}
elements.sent-2411-449: The details of $\varepsilon$ and $N_v$ for the SP method are summarized in Table~\ref{table:parametric_study_spec .}
elements.figure-2411-016: 4cm{!}{ \begin{tabular}{|c|c|} \text{$\varepsilon$} $N_v$ $0.1$ $50$, $100$, $200$, $400$, $800$ $0.2$ $50$, $100$, $200$, $400$, $800$ $0.5$ $20$, $40$, $80$, $160$, $320$ $1$ $10$, $20$, $40$, $80$, $160$ \end{tabular}} \caption{ Parameter settings for the SP method; velocity discretization is proportional to $1/\varepsilon$.} \label{table:parametric_study_spec}
elements.sent-2411-450: In Figure~\ref{figure:AE_SP_comparison , we present the log-plot of the relative $\ell_v^2$ errors of numerical solutions defined by \begin{equation}\label{equation:l2error} e_{Nv}(f) = \frac{\|f_{N_v} - f_{N_v/2}\|_{\ell_v^2}}{\|f_{N_v}\|_{\ell_v^2}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-451: Here $f_{N_v}$ denotes the numerical solution computed on a velocity grid of size $N_v \times N_v$, the slopes of each segment are plotted in the figure.
elements.sent-2411-452: We also record the computational time and orders of convergence for cases $\varepsilon = 0.1$ and $\varepsilon = 0.2$ in Table~\ref{table:time .}
elements.sent-2411-453: It is known that a numerical scheme is $k$-th order if $e_{N_v (f) \leq \frac{C {N_v^k}$ for $N_v \gg 1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-454: In Figure~\ref{figure:AE_SP_comparison , for the SP method, even though it is spectrally accurate, we observe that to maintain the same level of accuracy as $\varepsilon$ decreases, a velocity discretization proportional to $1/\varepsilon$ is necessary, which is consistent with our theoretical findings in Proposition \ref{Prop:SP .}}
elements.sent-2411-455: On the other hand, from Table~\ref{table:time we observe that, to achieve comparable accuracy for $\varepsilon = 0.1$ to that for $\varepsilon = 0.2$, the SP method requires a prohibitively growing computational cost.}
elements.sent-2411-456: On the contrary, the AE method exhibits two distinct properties.
elements.sent-2411-457: First, there is a slight deterioration of accuracy as $\varepsilon$ increases, which coincides with our expectations, since according to the truncation in~\eqref{equation:AE , the AE method fails to produce accurate results when $\varepsilon$ is large.}
elements.sent-2411-458: Second, the method exhibits similar order of accuracy as shown in the slope pairs $(f^L, f^H)$, by using the same $N_v$ values in different regimes, indicating that the method demonstrates a {\it uniform accuracy especially for $\varepsilon<0.1$.}
elements.sent-2411-459: From Table~\ref{table:time , we can see that the AE method has consistent computational cost across different regimes.}
elements.sent-2411-460: This behaviour of the numerical solutions benefits from the design of the AE method where $\varepsilon$ is decoupled from the collision operators.
elements.sent-2411-461: Lastly, in regimes with smaller $\varepsilon$, the AE method exhibits first-order accuracy for the distribution $f^L$ and second-order accuracy for the distribution $f^H$.
elements.sent-2411-462: We summarize our findings based on the above observations.
elements.sent-2411-463: The SP method achieves optimal performance for mixtures with nearly equal masses ($\varepsilon \approx 1$), whereas becomes computationally infeasible in disparate mass regimes due to almost not practical requirements in velocity discretization.
elements.sent-2411-464: On the contrary, the AE method demonstrates a uniformly cheap computational cost and works consistently efficient in disparate mass regimes where $\varepsilon \ll 1$ while maintaining the accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-465: This conclusion matches with our observation in Figure~\ref{figure:AE_SP_regimes , making the AE method a suitable candidate for gas mixture simulations in disparate mass regimes (when $\varepsilon \ll 1$).}

elements.sent-2411-468: In this test, we investigate if the truncation approach is accurate, with the SP method as the reference solution.
elements.sent-2411-469: We examine three moderate mass disparity regimes $\varepsilon = 0.2$, $\varepsilon = 0.1$, and $\varepsilon = 0.05$ corresponding to mass ratios $m_H/m_L = 25$, $100$, and $400$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-470: For each value of $\varepsilon$, we conduct simulations using the AE method with increasing velocity grid resolutions ($N_v = 20, 40, 80, 160, 320$).
elements.sent-2411-471: The reference solution computed by the SP method uses finer grids: $N_v = 320$ for $\varepsilon = 0.2$, $N_v = 640$ for $\varepsilon = 0.1$, and $N_v = 1280$ for $\varepsilon = 0.05$.

elements.sent-2411-519: In this paper, we have developed a new and efficient asymptotic-preserving (AP) scheme for the Boltzmann mixture model with disparate mass.
elements.sent-2411-520: A novel numerical method is constructed to compute the inter-particle collision operators on a delicate polar-grid mesh, upon truncating their asymptotic expansions.
elements.sent-2411-521: The proposed numerical scheme remains {\it uniformly accurate and computationally efficient for small mass ratio regimes.}
elements.sent-2411-522: We carefully design the AP time-stepping in the moment updating step of the scheme, in order to ensure stability while capturing the epochal relaxation phenomenon without resolving the small scaling parameter.
elements.sent-2411-523: A series of numerical examples have demonstrated the strength and efficiency of our AP scheme across a range of mass ratios.
elements.sent-2411-524: In future work, we will further address the accuracy and conservation preserving problem of the truncation method, in addition to study more general collision kernels and space-inhomogeneous problems.. \begin{appendices.}
